triples.head: Australian Small Businesses (SMBs)
triples.supporting_sentence: Turn on multi-factor authentication [is a recommended basic security measure].

triples.head: Australian Small Businesses (SMBs)
triples.supporting_sentence: Update your software [is a recommended basic security measure].

triples.head: Australian Small Businesses (SMBs)
triples.supporting_sentence: Back up your information [is a recommended basic security measure].

triples.head: Small businesses
triples.tail: Maturity Level One of the Essential Eight
triples.supporting_sentence: After completing this guide, we recommend small businesses implement Maturity Level One of the Essential Eight.

triples.head: Cyber security incident
triples.supporting_sentence: For a small business, even a minor cyber security incident can have devastating impacts.

triples.head: Cybercriminals
triples.supporting_sentence: Scams are a common way that cybercriminals target small businesses.

triples.head: Scam messages
triples.supporting_sentence: Their goal is to scam you or your staff into... giving away sensitive information, such as passwords.

triples.head: Scam messages
triples.supporting_sentence: Their goal is to scam you or your staff into: sending money or gift cards.

triples.head: Email attacks
triples.supporting_sentence: The Email attacks section provides advice for small businesses.

triples.head: Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
triples.supporting_sentence: One of the most effective ways to protect online accounts and applications from cyber criminals is through multi-factor authentication (MFA).

triples.head: Software updates
triples.supporting_sentence: Updates can fix security flaws in your operating system and other software, so that it is harder for a cybercriminal to break in.

triples.head: Regular backups
triples.supporting_sentence: Regular backups can help you recover your information if it is lost or compromised.

triples.head: Lack of regular backups
triples.tail: Impossible to recover information after a cyber attack
triples.supporting_sentence: Without a regular backup, it could be impossible for you to recover your information after a cyber attack.

triples.head: Security software
triples.supporting_sentence: Set up security software to complete regular scans on your devices.

triples.head: Cybercriminals
triples.tail: Devices and services in your network
triples.supporting_sentence: The devices and services in your network can be a prime target for cybercriminals.

triples.head: Administrative accounts (servers/NAS)
triples.supporting_sentence: Administrative accounts should be secured with a strong passphrase or multi-factor authentication.

triples.head: Cyber supply chain
triples.tail: Security issues with services or providers
triples.supporting_sentence: Security issues with these services or providers could have a significant impact to your business.

triples.head: Websites
triples.supporting_sentence: Websites are a prime target for cyber attacks.

triples.head: Regular website backups
triples.tail: Need to restore website after a cyber attack
triples.supporting_sentence: back up your website regularly so you can restore it after a cyber attack.

triples.head: Cybercriminals
triples.supporting_sentence: Data held by your business is an attractive target to cybercriminals.

triples.head: Data breaches
triples.supporting_sentence: Data breaches are on the rise – don’t let your business fall victim.

triples.head: Business data
triples.supporting_sentence: It’s important to understand what data your business holds, and in what locations.

triples.head: Decentralised data storage
triples.tail: More opportunities for a cybercriminal to attack
triples.supporting_sentence: Numerous systems can also create more opportunities for a cybercriminal to attack.

triples.head: Centralising data
triples.supporting_sentence: Centralising your data can create a bigger breach if your systems are compromised, so ensure this central location is adequately protected with secure configurations and restricted access.

triples.head: Employees
triples.tail: First line of defence against cyber attacks
triples.supporting_sentence: Employees with good cyber security practices are your first line of defence against cyber attacks.

triples.head: Small businesses
triples.tail: Emergency plan for cyber security incidents
triples.supporting_sentence: Create an emergency plan for cyber security incidents.

triples.head: ACSC Partnership Program
triples.supporting_sentence: Register your business with the ACSC Partnership Program.

triples.head: ACSC
triples.supporting_sentence: If you experience a ransomware incident and require support, call the ACSC’s 24/7 Hotline on 1300 CYBER1 (1300 292 371).

triples.head: Ransomware incidents
triples.supporting_sentence: victims are encouraged to report ransomware incidents to the ACSC at cyber.gov.au.

triples.head: Paying a ransom
triples.tail: Increased likelihood of being targeted again
triples.supporting_sentence: Paying a ransom does not guarantee a victim’s files will be restored... It also increases the likelihood of a victim being targeted again.

triples.head: ACSC Small Business Cyber Security Guide
triples.supporting_sentence: The language is clear, the actions are simple, and the guidance is tailored for small and medium businesses.

triples.head: ASD’s ACSC Small Business Cyber Security Guide
triples.supporting_sentence: This guide is intended to complement the detailed Australian Signals Directorate’s (ASD) Essential Eight maturity model.

triples.head: ASD’s ACSC Cyber Resources for Small Businesses
triples.supporting_sentence: ASD’s ACSC Cyber Resources for Small Businesses [is listed alongside] AISA Corporate Partnership and membership.

triples.head: SME Director
triples.supporting_sentence: director duties are relevant to the oversight of cyber security risks.

triples.head: SME Director
triples.tail: Being vigilant in updating computer systems, software, hardware, services, and applications
triples.supporting_sentence: An SME director should be confirming that the organisation is vigilant in updating computer systems, software, hardware, services, and applications to the latest versions.

triples.head: Unpatched vulnerabilities
triples.supporting_sentence: A large percentage of breaches are due to vulnerabilities that have not been patched, or vulnerabilities that have.

triples.head: Data stocktake
triples.tail: Visibility of the key data (customer, supplier, employee)
triples.supporting_sentence: Undertake a data stocktake that provides visibility of the key data that is collected from customers, suppliers and employees.

triples.head: Unique logins (for staff)
triples.supporting_sentence: Implement access and authorisation controls, including through creating unique logins for each staff member using computer systems.

triples.head: Cyber Incident Response Plan
triples.supporting_sentence: Having a documented Cyber Security Incident Response Plan is necessary for an organisation to respond swiftly, efficiently, and effectively to any incident, thereby minimising the impact.

triples.head: Business devices
triples.tail: Locked with a passphrase, PIN or biometrics
triples.supporting_sentence: Use security controls to further protect your business devices. At a minimum, they should be locked with a passphrase, PIN or biometrics.

triples.head: Devices
triples.tail: Automatically lock after a short time of inactivity
triples.supporting_sentence: Configure devices to automatically lock after a short time of inactivity.

triples.head: Shared accounts
triples.supporting_sentence: Limit the use of shared accounts and secure any that are used in your business.

triples.head: User access
triples.tail: Only what they need for their role
triples.supporting_sentence: Ensure each user can access only what they need for their role.

triples.head: Business devices (before disposal)
triples.supporting_sentence: Perform a factory reset before selling or disposing of business devices.

triples.head: SMBs
triples.tail: Less likely to implement cyber security measures
triples.supporting_sentence: Small businesses can be particularly vulnerable, as they are less likely to implement cyber security measures that could help prevent and recover from ransomware.

triples.head: ASD Essential Eight Mitigation Strategies
triples.supporting_sentence: implementing the Essential Eight Mitigation Strategies can be more cost-effective for SMBs in terms of time, money and effort than having to respond to a cyber security incident.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: Outsourcing IT security
triples.tail: Gaps in the owner’s cyber security knowledge
triples.supporting_sentence: Outsourcing may lead to gaps in the owner’s cyber security knowledge about which security measures are being implemented to protect their business.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: SMBs that outsource
triples.tail: Low implementation of Essential Eight Mitigation Strategies
triples.supporting_sentence: a large proportion of businesses who outsource have low implementation of the Essential Eight Mitigation Strategies.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: ACSC
triples.supporting_sentence: Technical advice must be prioritised so that Australian SMBs know which cyber security practices to implement first for best results.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: ACSC
triples.supporting_sentence: Guidance for Australian SMBs must be comprised of effective, low cost tips (in both time and money) that are easy to implement.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: Plain language advice/explanations of technical terms
triples.tail: Improved comprehension and retention for SMBs
triples.supporting_sentence: Plain language advice and explanations of technical terms... must be retained to improve comprehension and retention for SMBs.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: Ransomware
triples.supporting_sentence: Regularly backup your important data [is recommended] to Prevent and Recover from Ransomware.

triples.head: Ransomware
triples.tail: Automatically update operating systems, software and apps
triples.supporting_sentence: Automatically update your operating systems, software and apps [is recommended] to Prevent and Recover from Ransomware.

triples.head: Ransomware
triples.tail: Require multi-factor authentication to access services
triples.supporting_sentence: require multi-factor authentication to access services [is recommended] to Prevent and Recover from Ransomware.

triples.head: Operating system
triples.tail: Updated regularly to ensure most secure version
triples.supporting_sentence: your operating system is the most important piece of software on your computer... therefore needs to be updated regularly to ensure you are always using the most secure version.

triples.head: Backup storage device
triples.tail: Safely disconnecting and removing after each backup
triples.supporting_sentence: Safely disconnecting and removing your backup storage device after each backup will ensure it remains secure during a cyber incident.

triples.head: Backups
triples.tail: Testing regularly by attempting to restore data
triples.supporting_sentence: Test your backups regularly by attempting to restore data.

triples.head: One backup
triples.tail: Kept disconnected from your device, preferably offsite
triples.supporting_sentence: Always keep at least one backup disconnected from your device, preferably at an offsite location in case of natural disasters or theft.

triples.head: Backup
triples.tail: External storage device or to the cloud
triples.supporting_sentence: A backup is a digital copy of your business’ most important information e.g. customer details and financial records. This can be saved to an external storage device or to the cloud.

triples.head: Business data
triples.supporting_sentence: Data held by your business is an attractive target to cybercriminals. . . use the recommendations in this guide to help protect your data from being accessed by cybercriminals.

triples.head: Small businesses
triples.supporting_sentence: If you have questions about this advice or cyber security more broadly, we recommend you speak to an IT professional or a trusted advisor.

triples.head: Small businesses
triples.tail: Consulting with a legal professional (for legal obligations)
triples.supporting_sentence: Consult with a legal professional if you are unsure [of legal obligations for data].

triples.head: Small businesses
triples.tail: Understanding obligations for protecting personal information
triples.supporting_sentence: Know your obligations for protecting data. Some small businesses may have legal obligations for handling personal information they collect.

triples.head: Cyber security is important
triples.supporting_sentence: Australian SMBs know cyber security is important regardless of how they rate their understanding of cyber security.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: Ransomware attacks
triples.tail: Hackers demanding money for unlocking data
triples.supporting_sentence: There have been many reports of systems and even backups being maliciously encrypted, with hackers demanding hundreds and thousands of dollars for the ‘privilege’ of unlocking important data and systems.

triples.head: NAS or other servers
triples.supporting_sentence: These devices are common targets for cybercriminals because they often store important files or perform important functions.

triples.head: Cyber security practices
triples.supporting_sentence: Cyber risks and cyber security best practices change over time, so learning must be ongoing with cyber risks and behaviours continually monitored.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: ACSC guidance
triples.supporting_sentence: Guidance for Australian SMBs must be comprised of effective, low cost tips (in both time and money) that are easy to implement.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: Automating updates and backups
triples.tail: Cyber security practices decay over time
triples.supporting_sentence: This will ensure cyber security practices are both implemented and maintained over time.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: Case studies (in advice)
triples.tail: Better assess cyber risks and prioritise practices
triples.supporting_sentence: Case studies should be used in cyber security advice because it will help Australian SMBs to better assess cyber risks and prioritise cyber security practices.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: Time poor SMBs
triples.tail: Guidance that covers automating updates and backups
triples.supporting_sentence: Guidance must also provide options that make it easier for time poor SMBs to stay on top of things, for example guidance that covers automating updates and backups.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: ASD’s ACSC Small Business Cyber Security Guide
triples.supporting_sentence: This guidance is taken from the ASD’s ACSC Small Business Cyber Security Guide [and provides] PASSWORD AND PASSPHRASE BEST PRACTICE.

triples.head: IT asset inventory
triples.supporting_sentence: Maintaining an asset list facilitates efficient tracking and awareness of the technology endpoints present in your business.

triples.head: Cyber risk
triples.supporting_sentence: Cyber risk, despite its prominence and velocity, is still an operational risk that should fit within an SME’s existing approach to risk management.

triples.head: SME Director
triples.tail: Setting tone from the top (culture of cyber resilience)
triples.supporting_sentence: Directors have a key role in setting a tone from the [top] [to build a culture of cyber resilience].

triples.head: Best practice backups
triples.supporting_sentence: Best practice is for these back-ups to be in a separate location which can be accessed when the original form [is compromised].

triples.head: Cybersecurity awareness training
triples.supporting_sentence: Employees with good cyber security practices are your first line of defence against cyber attacks.

triples.head: ACSC
triples.supporting_sentence: ANZ in collaboration with the Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC) is set to play a joint role in securing the digital future for the Small and Medium Businesses.

triples.head: Phishing
triples.supporting_sentence: Malware, Phishing and Ransomware are the most commonly understood cyber security risks.
triples.source_document: 2023_ACSC_Cyber Security and Australian Small Businesses Survey Results_D1.pdf

triples.head: Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC)
triples.supporting_sentence: Read the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner’s guide for small businesses to learn more, available at oaic.gov.au.

triples.head: Notifiable Data Breaches (NDB) scheme
triples.tail: Organizations subject to material data breach
triples.supporting_sentence: The Notifiable Data Breaches (NDB) scheme – requiring an organisation to notify affected individuals and the Office of the Australian Information Commissioner (OAIC) as soon as practicable of a material data breach.

triples.head: Australian Privacy Principle 11 (APP 11)
triples.tail: Active measures to ensure the security of personal information
triples.supporting_sentence: Australian Privacy Principle 11... requiring an organisation to take active measures to ensure the security of personal information it holds.

triples.head: Cyber Incident Response Planning
triples.supporting_sentence: How well an organisation can respond to a cyber security incident depends on their preparedness.

triples.head: Security issues with outsourced services
triples.supporting_sentence: Security issues with these services or providers could have a significant impact to your business.

triples.head: Outsourced services
triples.supporting_sentence: Modern businesses often outsource multiple services. For example, using a Managed Service Provider to maintain their IT.

triples.head: ASD’s ACSC Quick Wins Guides
triples.supporting_sentence: The ACSC has additional resources available for website owners. Search for these resources on cyber.gov.au: Quick Wins for your Website.

triples.head: ASD’s ACSC Step-by-Step Guides
triples.tail: Backing Up & Restoring Your Computer (For Mac)
triples.supporting_sentence: Step-by-Step Guide Backing Up & Restoring Your Computer (For Mac).

triples.head: ASD’s ACSC Small Business Cyber Security Guide
triples.supporting_sentence: Content Complexity SIMPLE # Small business cyber security guide.

triples.head: ACSC
triples.supporting_sentence: For detailed advice on backing up your business, read our advice for backups, available at cyber.gov.au/backups.

triples.head: ACSC
triples.supporting_sentence: For detailed advice on cyber supply chain risk management read our Cyber Supply Chain Guidance on cyber.gov.au.

triples.head: Cybercriminals
triples.supporting_sentence: secure your website login with multi-factor authentication or a strong password.

triples.head: Antivirus software
triples.tail: ACSC advice on alternative products and options
triples.supporting_sentence: For alternative products and options, read our advice on antivirus software, by searching antivirus on cyber.gov.au.

triples.head: Password manager
triples.tail: Create and store unique passwords or passphrases
triples.supporting_sentence: Use a password manager to create and store unique passwords or passphrases for each of your important accounts.

triples.head: Virus
triples.supporting_sentence: A program designed to cause damage, steal personal information, modify data, send email, display messages or a combination of these actions.

triples.head: Ransomware/Viruses
triples.supporting_sentence: Do not connect your backup to devices that are infected with ransomware or viruses.

triples.head: MFA
triples.tail: Combination of something you know and something you have
triples.supporting_sentence: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) typically requires a combination of: something you know (password/passphrase, PIN, secret question) [and] something you have (e.g. phone or token).

triples.head: Business data
triples.supporting_sentence: Understand the data your business holds and your responsibilities to protect it.

triples.head: Small businesses
triples.tail: Reading ACSC resources on website security
triples.supporting_sentence: Read through the Australian Cyber Security Centre (ACSC) resources on website security.
